text:
MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD CONSULTATION NOTE
Medical Oncology / Genitourinary Oncology

**PATIENT:** Raymond Kowalski
**REFERRING SERVICE:** Urology / Surgical Oncology
**REASON FOR REFERRAL:** Newly diagnosed metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma; multidisciplinary management planning

---

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with a new diagnosis of metastatic right-sided clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) presenting for multidisciplinary tumor board review and medical oncology consultation to establish a comprehensive treatment plan.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old retired industrial engineer with a 30 pack-year smoking history (quit 15 years ago), hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. He presented approximately four weeks ago with a three-week history of gross hematuria, right-sided flank pain, and approximately 15 pounds of unintentional weight loss over the preceding three months. Initial work-up by his primary care physician included urinalysis confirming hematuria and a comprehensive metabolic panel notable for mildly elevated serum calcium at 10.9 mg/dL. He was promptly referred to urology for further evaluation.

Cross-sectional imaging was obtained. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast demonstrated a large, heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.1 cm arising from the upper and mid pole with central necrosis, right renal vein involvement without definite extension into the inferior vena cava, and multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 1.4 cm in the right lower lobe) consistent with metastatic disease. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was identified, with a 2.8 cm right para-aortic node and a 1.9 cm aortocaval node. No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastases were identified on initial staging CT.

Subsequent MRI of the abdomen with and without contrast was performed for pre-surgical planning and confirmed the right renal mass with a renal vein thrombus extending to but not into the inferior vena cava (IVC). No IVC thrombus was identified. Gerota fascia was intact. The right adrenal gland appeared uninvolved. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was confirmed.

Given the appearance of the mass, a CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right renal mass was performed. Final pathology was diagnostic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation estimated at 20% of the sampled tissue. Immunohistochemistry was notable for: PAX8 positive, CA-IX diffusely positive, CD10 positive, vimentin positive, CK7 focally positive, TFE3 negative. Ki-67 proliferation index was elevated at 35%, confirming highly proliferative disease. PD-L1 (SP142) combined positive score was 15, tumor proportion score 10%.

Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) was performed on the biopsy specimen and returned with actionable and co-occurring alterations: VHL p.L89fs*4 (loss of function, VAF 48%), PBRM1 p.Q357* (loss of function, VAF 42%), BAP1 p.R227* (loss of function, VAF 39%), SETD2 p.R1625* (VAF 31%), KDM5C p.Y611* (VAF 22%), CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification (copy number 6.8), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 18%), and a TERT promoter C228T mutation (VAF 35%). Chromosome 3p loss encompassing VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, and SETD2 loci was confirmed on copy number analysis. Tumor mutational burden was low at 6.2 mutations/Mb; microsatellite status was stable (MSS). PD-L1 by 22C3 returned a CPS of 18. No TSC1/TSC2, HIF2A, FGFR, or NF2 alterations were identified. The co-occurring PBRM1 and BAP1 loss was flagged by the reporting laboratory as associated with higher-grade histology and sarcomatoid dedifferentiation, consistent with this patient's pathology.

The formal IMDC (International Metastatic RCC Database Consortium) risk assessment was performed:
- Less than one year from diagnosis to first systemic therapy: YES
- Hemoglobin below lower limit of normal: YES (Hgb 10.2 g/dL)
- Corrected calcium above upper limit of normal: BORDERLINE (serum calcium 10.9 mg/dL, elevated)
- KPS < 80: NO (KPS 80)
- Thrombocytosis: YES (platelets 478,000/uL)
- Neutrophil count above upper limit of normal: NO

IMDC risk: Intermediate (2 confirmed factors: <1 year from diagnosis to treatment; thrombocytosis). Additional adverse features include grade 4 histology, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, and BAP1 loss -- none formally part of the IMDC model but biologically relevant.

LDH: 287 U/L (mildly elevated). NLR: 4.2.

The case was formally presented at the genitourinary multidisciplinary tumor board today, with representation from medical oncology, urology/surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and diagnostic radiology.

---

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**

1. Hypertension -- longstanding, managed with lisinopril
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- managed with metformin, HbA1c last checked ~8 months ago (unknown to this reviewer)
3. Former smoker -- 30 pack-year history, quit 15 years ago
4. No prior history of cardiac disease, pulmonary disease, prior malignancy, or autoimmune conditions
5. No prior surgeries documented aside from this current workup

---

**FAMILY HISTORY**

Father: deceased, history of coronary artery disease. Mother: deceased, history of type 2 diabetes. No known family history of renal cell carcinoma, hereditary cancer syndromes (von Hippel-Lindau, hereditary papillary RCC, Birt-Hogg-Dube, tuberous sclerosis complex), or other malignancies. No first-degree relatives with kidney cancer.

---

**SOCIAL HISTORY**

Retired industrial engineer; reports being previously very active in his occupation, now largely sedentary since diagnosis due to fatigue and discomfort. Former smoker, 30 pack-year history, quit 15 years ago; no current tobacco use. Denies alcohol use beyond occasional social drinking (1-2 drinks per week). No illicit drug use. Married; lives with spouse. Has two adult children. Insurance coverage confirmed (per referral documentation). No known environmental exposures beyond occupational (industrial engineering -- some solvent and heavy metal exposure historically, though not clearly delineated).

---

**ALLERGIES**

No known drug allergies. No food allergies documented.

---

**MEDICATIONS**

1. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes mellitus)
3. No supplements, herbal preparations, or over-the-counter medications reported

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

Positive: gross hematuria (ongoing at presentation, now lessening per patient), right-sided flank pain (moderate, 5/10, constant), significant unintentional weight loss (~15 lbs over 3 months), generalized fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance, decreased appetite. Borderline: mild dyspnea on exertion (patient attributes to deconditioning and anemia). Negative: no hemoptysis, no chest pain, no palpitations, no cough, no neurological symptoms (headache, focal weakness, vision changes), no bone pain, no jaundice, no nausea or vomiting, no change in bowel habits, no dysuria beyond hematuria.

---

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**

General: Alert and oriented, well-appearing male in no acute distress. Appears stated age. Mildly cachectic. Weight: 198 lbs. Height: 5'10". BSA approximately 2.0 m2.

Vital signs: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg (right arm), heart rate 82 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 98.4 F, SpO2 97% on room air.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. No pallor of conjunctivae appreciated. Oral mucosa moist, no lesions.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or crackles.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender to palpation. A large, non-tender right-sided flank/abdominal mass is palpable. No peritoneal signs. No splenomegaly. No left-sided mass. Normal bowel sounds.

Genitourinary: No costovertebral angle tenderness on the left. Right CVA tenderness present, consistent with known mass.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. No calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses intact.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x 4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits.

Skin: No rashes, no telangiectasias, no stigmata of paraneoplastic syndromes.

Lymph nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy detected (no inguinal, axillary, or supraclavicular nodes).

---

**LABORATORY RESULTS (RECENT)**

Complete blood count:
- WBC 9.8 x10^3/uL (normal differential)
- Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (normochromic, normocytic anemia)
- Hematocrit 31.4%
- Platelets 478,000/uL (thrombocytosis)

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 101 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
- BUN 22 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~67 mL/min/1.73m2)
- Calcium 10.9 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
- Total protein 6.8 g/dL, Albumin 3.6 g/dL
- AST 26 U/L, ALT 29 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 98 U/L

Additional labs:
- LDH 287 U/L (upper limit of normal ~240 U/L; mildly elevated)
- TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
- HbA1c: pending at this visit
- NLR: 4.2
- Urinalysis: 3+ blood, no WBCs, no casts
- PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)

---

**IMAGING RESULTS (SUMMARY FOR TUMOR BOARD)**

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging): Large 11.4 cm heterogeneous right renal mass with central necrosis, upper/mid pole origin, right renal vein involvement without IVC extension. Bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 1.4 cm RLL; additional 0.8 cm LLL and 0.6 cm RUL nodules), consistent with metastatic disease. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (2.8 cm right para-aortic, 1.9 cm aortocaval). No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastases.

MRI abdomen with/without contrast (pre-surgical planning): Confirms right renal vein thrombus extending to but not beyond the IVC confluence. No IVC involvement. Gerota fascia intact. Right adrenal gland spared. Retroperitoneal adenopathy confirmed.

Biopsy pathology: Clear cell RCC, WHO/ISUP grade 4, sarcomatoid differentiation ~20%. PAX8+, CA-IX diffuse+, TFE3-. Ki-67 35%. PD-L1 (SP142) CPS 15.

NGS (Foundation One CDx): VHL loss (frameshift), PBRM1 loss (nonsense), BAP1 loss (nonsense -- co-occurring with PBRM1, associated with aggressive biology), SETD2 loss, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification (CN 6.8), TP53 R248W, TERT promoter mutation. Chromosome 3p loss, 9p loss, 14q loss, 5q gain. TMB-low, MSS. PD-L1 (22C3) CPS 18.

---

**MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION**

The case of Mr. Kowalski was reviewed in detail at today's GU tumor board. Present disciplines: GU Medical Oncology (attending), Urology/Surgical Oncology (attending), Radiation Oncology (attending), Interventional Radiology, and GU Diagnostic Radiology (attending). The following key discussion points were addressed:

1. **Stage and risk stratification**: Stage IV (cT3bN1M1) ccRCC by AJCC 8th edition. IMDC Intermediate risk (2 factors). Biologically high-risk features include grade 4 histology, 20-22% sarcomatoid differentiation, BAP1 loss, and CDKN2A/B deletion. These genomic features are associated with aggressive disease behavior and poor prognosis on historical data but are not yet incorporated into risk-stratification models.

2. **Role of cytoreductive nephrectomy (CN)**: The question of upfront CN versus systemic therapy first was debated. Key considerations: (a) The CARMENA trial showed non-inferiority of Sutent alone vs. CN + suni in IMDC intermediate/poor risk patients; however, this data does not directly apply to the immunotherapy era. (b) This patient has an IMDC intermediate risk score (2 factors), has reasonable performance status (KPS 80, ECOG 1), and has limited metastatic burden -- oligometastatic pulmonary disease and regional retroperitoneal adenopathy. The primary tumor is large and symptomatic (gross hematuria, flank pain). (c) Nephrectomy was felt to offer meaningful tumor burden reduction (primary is the dominant site of disease by volume), symptom palliation (ongoing hematuria, flank discomfort), and definitive pathologic characterization of the sarcomatoid component. (d) The surgical oncology team confirmed technical feasibility; renal vein thrombus is manageable with planned open right radical nephrectomy. IVC is spared. (e) Urology/surgical oncology team recommends open cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with en bloc Gerota fascia excision, right adrenalectomy, and template retroperitoneal lymph node dissection given nodal involvement. The board consensus is in favor of CN as the initial step.

3. **Planned systemic therapy post-CN**: Given IMDC intermediate risk, availability of immunotherapy combination data (CheckMate 214), and the elevated PD-L1 expression on both biopsy (CPS 15) and NGS assay (CPS 18 by 22C3), and particularly given the sarcomatoid differentiation -- a histologic feature shown in the CheckMate 214 subgroup analysis and subsequently confirmed in broader analyses to derive the most robust and durable benefit from Yervoy plus nivo compared to Sutent -- the board strongly favors Yervoy (ipi) 1 mg/kg + Opdivo (nivo) 3 mg/kg IV every three weeks for four induction cycles, followed by nivo 480 mg flat dosing every four weeks as maintenance. This regimen (ipi/nivo, the Opdivo-Yervoy combination in RCC) has demonstrated superior OS, PFS, and ORR over Sutent in IMDC intermediate/poor risk patients, with the sarcomatoid subgroup showing particularly compelling responses (ORR ~57% in sarcomatoid component data). The MET amplification on NGS is noted; though not a primary driver of treatment selection in the first-line setting, it is flagged as potentially clinically relevant if second-line VEGFR/MET inhibitor therapy (e.g., cabozantinib, which inhibits VEGFR2, MET, and AXL) is ultimately required.

4. **Role of radiation**: Radiation oncology reviewed the case. No role for upfront radiotherapy identified. Pulmonary nodules are small and not causing symptoms. No CNS involvement. Radiation is held in reserve for palliation or stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) if oligoprogression develops after systemic therapy.

5. **Bone health and ancillary assessments**: No evidence of osseous metastases on current staging CT. Bone-directed therapy not indicated at this time. Endocrine evaluation and diabetes management optimization recommended pre-surgery (metformin should be held perioperatively per standard protocol given renal implications). HbA1c pending.

---

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

**Assessment**: Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with newly diagnosed stage IV (cT3bN1M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma, IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors), WHO/ISUP grade 4 with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, PD-L1 positive (CPS 18 by 22C3), with VHL/PBRM1/BAP1 co-mutation, MET amplification, and a high-risk molecular profile. His disease is oligometastatic at presentation (bilateral pulmonary nodules, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy) with a large, symptomatic primary tumor involving the right renal vein.

**Plan**:

1. **Cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection**: Recommended by multidisciplinary consensus. Open approach planned by urology/surgical oncology. Anticipated R0 resection of primary. Renal vein thrombus to be excised. Right adrenalectomy included. Patient to be seen by surgical oncology for pre-operative clearance and counseling; surgery targeted within 2-3 weeks.

2. **Pre-operative optimization**: Hold metformin 48 hours prior to and 48 hours following surgical procedure and contrast administration. Lisinopril to be held day of surgery and restarted when hemodynamically stable and creatinine reassessed. CBC, CMP, coagulation studies to be repeated pre-operatively. Anesthesia pre-operative assessment to be scheduled.

3. **Post-operative systemic therapy (planned, pending recovery)**: Yervoy 1 mg/kg IV + Opdivo 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks x 4 induction cycles, then Opdivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. To be initiated approximately 4-6 weeks post-nephrectomy pending adequate wound healing, renal function stability, and performance status recovery. Regimen to be formally consented at post-operative oncology visit.

4. **Patient counseling today**: Extensive counseling provided regarding overall disease extent (stage IV, metastatic), prognosis (median OS in IMDC intermediate risk on ipi/nivo approximately 28 months in CheckMate 214, with a meaningful proportion achieving durable long-term responses), and treatment plan rationale. Immunotherapy immune-related adverse event profile discussed at a high level (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathy -- particularly hypophysitis, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, and type 1 diabetes -- dermatitis). Patient is engaged, asking appropriate questions, accompanied by spouse today. Expressed understanding and agreement with the proposed treatment plan.

5. **Supportive care and monitoring**: Anemia -- monitor trend post-operatively; anticipated improvement following nephrectomy given paraneoplastic contribution. Hypercalcemia -- mild, does not require immediate bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy; will reassess post-operatively. Diabetes management -- endocrinology referral placed for perioperative optimization and to clarify HbA1c status.

6. **Next medical oncology visit**: Scheduled approximately 4 weeks post-nephrectomy for post-surgical assessment, repeat laboratory evaluation (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH baseline prior to immunotherapy initiation), and formal treatment consent for ipi/nivo.

7. **Tumor board documentation**: Case formally entered into the GU multidisciplinary tumor board registry. Consensus recommendation documented for cytoreductive nephrectomy followed by ipi/Opdivo induction and nivo maintenance.

---

**Attending Physician, GU Medical Oncology**

Note reviewed and electronically signed.

CC: Urology/Surgical Oncology, Primary Care Physician, Radiation Oncology
Time spent: 75 minutes total (including tumor board review, patient interview, and documentation).
